title: NX-OS Control Plane Policing (CoPP) Drops
dataSources: NX-OS CoPP counters (`show policy-map interface control-plane`), syslog, gNMI
app: NX-OS syslog (`cisco:nexus`), SNMP TA, gNMI telemetry
spl:
index=network sourcetype="cisco:nexus:copp" OR (sourcetype="cisco:nexus" "COPP" "DROP")
| stats sum(dropped_packets) as drops sum(conform_packets) as conforms by host, class_name
| eval drop_pct=round(drops/(drops+conforms)*100,2)
| where drops > 1000 OR drop_pct > 5
| table host, class_name, drops, conforms, drop_pct
| sort -drops
description: CoPP protects the switch CPU from being overwhelmed by excessive control-plane traffic (ARP storms, BGP attacks, ICMP floods). Monitoring CoPP drop counters detects both legitimate overload and potential DoS attacks targeting the management or routing plane.
value: CoPP protects the switch CPU from being overwhelmed by excessive control-plane traffic (ARP storms, BGP attacks, ICMP floods). Monitoring CoPP drop counters detects both legitimate overload and potential DoS attacks targeting the management or routing plane.
implementation: Poll CoPP counters via scripted input or gNMI every 60 seconds. Baseline normal drop rates per class. Alert on sustained drops exceeding baseline, particularly for BGP, OSPF, and management classes. Investigate as potential security events.
visualization: Table (CoPP classes with drops), Bar chart (drops by class), Line chart (drop rate trending).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: NX-OS syslog (`cisco:nexus`), SNMP TA, gNMI telemetry.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: NX-OS CoPP counters (`show policy-map interface control-plane`), syslog, gNMI.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Poll CoPP counters via scripted input or gNMI every 60 seconds. Baseline normal drop rates per class. Alert on sustained drops exceeding baseline, particularly for BGP, OSPF, and management classes. Investigate as potential security events.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=network sourcetype="cisco:nexus:copp" OR (sourcetype="cisco:nexus" "COPP" "DROP")
| stats sum(dropped_packets) as drops sum(conform_packets) as conforms by host, class_name
| eval drop_pct=round(drops/(drops+conforms)*100,2)
| where drops > 1000 OR drop_pct > 5
| table host, class_name, drops, conforms, drop_pct
| sort -drops
```

Understanding this SPL

**NX-OS Control Plane Policing (CoPP) Drops** — CoPP protects the switch CPU from being overwhelmed by excessive control-plane traffic (ARP storms, BGP attacks, ICMP floods). Monitoring CoPP drop counters detects both legitimate overload and potential DoS attacks targeting the management or routing plane.

Documented **Data sources**: NX-OS CoPP counters (`show policy-map interface control-plane`), syslog, gNMI. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): NX-OS syslog (`cisco:nexus`), SNMP TA, gNMI telemetry. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: network; **sourcetype**: cisco:nexus:copp, cisco:nexus. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=network, sourcetype="cisco:nexus:copp". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, class_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **drop_pct** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where drops > 1000 OR drop_pct > 5` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **NX-OS Control Plane Policing (CoPP) Drops**): table host, class_name, drops, conforms, drop_pct
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm results in the index, then compare with the same scope in the relevant fabric/SDN controller or manager UI.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (CoPP classes with drops), Bar chart (drops by class), Line chart (drop rate trending).

Scripted input (generic example)
This use case relies on a scripted input. In the app's local/inputs.conf add a stanza such as:

```ini
[script://$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/YourApp/bin/collect.sh]
interval = 300
sourcetype = your_sourcetype
index = main
disabled = 0
```

The script should print one event per line (e.g. key=value). Example minimal script (bash):

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Output metrics or events, one per line
echo "metric=value timestamp=$(date +%s)"
```

For full details (paths, scheduling, permissions), see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md
grandmaExplanation: CoPP protects the switch CPU from being overwhelmed by excessive control-plane traffic (ARP storms, attacks, floods) — so traffic problems, routing mistakes, and risky changes surface fast.
knownFalsePositives: Faults during planned maintenance, hardware refresh, or after firmware upgrades.
